## [00:00 - 03:03]

Okay, so this section of the secrets of leverage and scale is all about the big picture. This is the advanced stuff. This is how to get the life that you want. So the first thing I want to talk about is what I consider probably the most important thing in business, and that is opportunity cost. And so opportunity cost is the cost that you will have when you take on a project. What will taking on that project prevent you from doing? And often in pure business world, the opportunity cost is thought of as a financial thing. Like if you spend this million dollars to create this project, what product projects do you have to forego that you could have created with that million dollars? So I go and I spend this million dollars to create this great restaurant. What else could I have done with that million dollars? But in our kinds of businesses, which are generally not as capital intensive but more time intensive, the same thing applies. In fact, more so, because time is more limited than financial resources. And so if you take this project on, what project will you not be able to take on? What project are you giving up by taking this project on? If you take this client on, what are the clients are you going to have to say no to? There's this quote I love by Warren Buffett, who is probably the most successful investor ever. He's a billionaire many, many times over. And he says that very successful people, the difference between successful people and very successful business people, let me try this again. The difference between successful people and really successful people is that really successful people say no to almost everything. Now saying no doesn't come easy to very many of us. To most of us, we have a hard time saying no. But that's the thing you always have to say, what is the opportunity cost here? My friend, Dean Greziosi, he thinks about, he's sitting in his office and behind him is a bookcase of his favorite books. He just loves these books. He cherishes these books. And the bookcase is completely full. Someone comes in and they have just a wonderful book, or a book they're trying to sell you, or they're trying to give you, or they're trying to get you to consume. And he looks at that and says, if I take this book, I'm going to have to turn it around to my bookcase,

## [03:03 - 06:10]

and I'm going to have to take one of those books and throw it away. And of course, the books are opportunities. So someone comes in and says, here, I've got this great opportunity for you. I've got this great project that you can take on. I need your help with this project. Then he has to take a look at all his other projects and potential projects and throw one of those away. Or he has to say no. And that's why saying no is so critical, because the number of opportunities that come your way, there's no end to the number of opportunities that come your way. And as you become more successful in business, then your deal flow, the number of possible deals that are coming to you will skyrocket. So opportunity cost is, in my opinion, the most important thing in business. And as your business grows, it will become more important. Your time becomes, the pressures on your time become greater. The potential for your time to become fractured become greater. More people want a piece of you. They keep on bringing more things in front of you. Your time is more valuable. In a very direct financial sense, and just, you're getting older. That's the way it works. As you become more successful, you're also getting older, and times moving faster and it's ever more fleeting. So you have to get really good at saying no. And I know, I can remember, this is a long time ago, when my income was a fraction of what it is today. And at some point I said, you know, any new project comes along, it has to bring at least $50,000 to me. And this is way early days. This has to bring $50,000 to me, like almost assuredly, before I even start to think about it. And that was back when I was a lot more financially motivated than I am now. So you have to say no. It is a critical skill for you to develop. It's the ability to say no. And that ability, and that just becomes more important as your business grows. Because the deal flow, the amount of possible deals coming in, will just explode as you become more successful. One of the things that you might consider is focusing on what's the value of a focus day. So a focus day is something I learned from Dan Sullivan. And what he tries to set up his days so that he has free days where he's not working on anything, anything business related. And then he also has buffer days. And that's where he does sort of the get stuck doing nitty gritty, so to speak, of the business. The pain in the neck stuff that all of us seem stuck doing, you know, the filing and the taxes and administrative stuff.

## [06:10 - 09:11]

And hopefully as your business grows and your team grows, you do less than less of that. But it's hard to completely eliminate it 100%. So he has free days and buffer days. And hopefully there aren't very many buffer days, but then there's also focus days. And that's where you're doing your genius work. This is all Dan Sullivan 101. And one of the things that's valuable is to calculate what's the value of a focus day. Because the reality is you don't have that many focus days in a year. You know, if you get, holy cow, if you can get 100 focus days in a year, or 150 focus days would be pretty heroic in my opinion. Let's just say you get somewhere between 100 and 150 focus days a year. How much is one of those days worth? These are the days where you don't have any distractions and you're purely doing your genius work. For me, that's creating marketing, it's creating content, creating training like I'm doing right now. Or it's building relationships with my partners. So those are like the three primary focus day activities I've got. And what is the value of an entire day spent on those focus activities? Those make more activities. So what you can do is you can, I mean, there's lots of different ways that you can calculate this. But if you just sit down and say, I have 100 of those days a year. And those are the days that drive the money. So those are the money making days. This is how much money I make in a year. And then you simply define it by how many focus days you get in a year. And you can start to get a picture of what is a focus day worth. And it's probably worth a lot more than you think. Because let's just say that you work, you take weekends off. Okay, so you take weekends off. So that means there's 52 weekends in a year. So that's 104 days. So you're down to about 260 days in a year. Then let's say you take off three weeks. So now you're, to make the math easy, let's say you take off four weeks a year. Now as an entrepreneur, you might be an overworked entrepreneur, and you might not take off any days. You might not even take off weekends. You might work 365 days a year. I hope you don't, but there are some people like that. But let's say you take out weekends and four weeks a year. It takes you down to 150 days. I think it does. It takes you down to 250. I shouldn't be doing math here while I'm recording. But let's say that takes you, the weekends would take you down to 250 days a year.

## [09:11 - 12:14]

Take off 20 days. You're down to 230 days a year. Maybe 80 of those days are buffer days. So now you're down to 150 days a year. So take whatever your income is and divide it by 150, and then you get the value of a focus day. And you can even drop that down and look at what the value of a focus hour is. And now you can start to understand how important those days are. Or maybe you've got a job, and so you're working that job, so that makes your time in your business even much, much, much shorter. And now you can start to say, you might only have 20 focus days a year. But it's important for you to just start to quantify what a focus day is worth, and maybe even what a focus hour is worth. Because that will help you guide you, that will help guide you in your decision making, and you can start to say, it might get a little easier to say no to those projects that are just not going to create the income and lifestyle that you want. Another thing that focuses on is how much are you making? When we're just talking about dollars, how much are you... A lot of times, especially when you're around a bunch of marketers, you'll hear them talking about sales, the sales dollars. But it's important to track your sales profit. Look at what your profit is. Now there are times where sales can be really important. Sometimes where the sales are more important than others, maybe for positioning or for market power. It can bring you... These days, if you look at something like Amazon, where they're more about sales than they are about profits, there's a lot of the newer tech companies that are like that, and they're trying to amass this huge market share and this huge market positioning. But in our kinds of businesses, it usually pays to focus on the profit on the bottom line. So remember, sales is sort of a vanity. Often it's a vanity number, but the profit's the important number. And so always be looking at your projects and what you're taking on with the idea of not just of sales, but also profit. I know that sounds like a really simple thing, but a lot of people screw that up. And then the number... Throughout all of this, you really just have to focus on what do you want in your life? You can sit down and define what do you want in your life? What do you value in your life? I am driven to make an impact, to help people out. But I'm also driven to have a great lifestyle, to be able to step away from my computer, to be able to have fun, to be able to get in the wilderness.

## [12:14 - 14:20]

I love being in the wilderness. I love being in the wilderness for extended trips, so I don't want a business where I have to be on call or I have to be available all the time, where I can't afford to take off two or three or four weeks and be completely disconnected. Now, there aren't that many people that value that. I think I'm a bit of an oddball there. You have to figure out what do you value? What do you want in your life? What do you want your life to look like? I went through this process. It was called Life Book. It's a program called Life Book. It was a great, great program. And it had you walk through 12 areas of your life and define what you want your life to look like in that area when it comes to health, when it comes to emotional health, physical health, mental health, when it comes to family relationships, when it comes to parenting relationships, when it comes to your romantic relationship, when it comes to your financial success, when it comes to your business success. In every one of those areas, you sit down and they had a process to do this, and you might want to check it out. It's called Life Book. I think it's at MyLifeBook, it's the website. Sit down and design what you want in your life. And then when you make these decisions in your business, you should make them in alignment with what you want in your life. You signed up to be an entrepreneur. That means you're creating. You're working hard. You might as well create what you want in your life. Create the life you want. Don't live by someone else's rules. You write the rules that you want. I mean, as long as they're legal and ethical, write the rules that you want. Create the rules that you want that are going to give you the life that you want. And then build that business. That gives you that.
